ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT code 102263004 refers to edible eggs as a substance; no specific hard-boiled egg preparation code available; closest_parent used.

serving.description: 2 medium hard-boiled eggs (approximately 100 g edible portion)
serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is 2 medium hard-boiled hen eggs, approximately 100 g total edible weight, seasoned with salt, pepper, and chilli powder.

nutrient_source.title: Eggs, hard-boiled — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy, NDB No. 01129
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for hard-boiled eggs (NDB 01129 / FDC ID 173424) used as primary nutrient source. Values represent the plain hard-boiled egg; added salt, pepper, and chilli powder contribute negligible macronutrient change at typical sprinkle quantities. Sodium adjusted upward slightly to reflect light salting consistent with the dish description; base sodium from USDA is 124 mg/100 g for hard-boiled eggs.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association; University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Eggs have a glycemic index of effectively zero because they contain negligible digestible carbohydrate (1.12 g/100 g, predominantly glycoprotein-bound). The University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283) confirm eggs as a zero-GI food. Per the Veritas 'Zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule' (which applies to all effectively zero-GI foods), glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel value) and gi_category is 'low'. glycemic_load_per_serving is calculated as (GI * carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100, which for 100g serving is (1 * 1.12) / 100 = 0.0112, rounded to 0.0.

hypertension.guidance: This dish contains approximately 250 mg sodium per 100 g due to added salt; individuals with hypertension should request minimal or no added salt and limit portion to one egg to keep sodium intake within daily targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and zero glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hard-boiled eggs have a glycemic index of effectively zero and only 1.12 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making them an excellent protein-rich food choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes with minimal impact on postprandial blood glucose.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol (373 mg/100 g) and saturated fat (3.27 g/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Eggs contain 373 mg cholesterol per 100 g; current evidence suggests moderate egg consumption (up to one egg per day) is acceptable for most individuals with cardiovascular disease, but those with dyslipidaemia should consult a dietitian regarding frequency.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Boiled Eggs (2 pieces) is a simple, protein-rich dish served at Indian restaurants in Singapore, notably at establishments such as Zaffron Tandoori. Two medium hen eggs are hard-boiled until fully set, then served whole or halved and sprinkled with salt, ground black pepper, and chilli powder, giving the dish a mildly spiced flavour profile characteristic of Indian-influenced egg preparations. The texture is firm throughout — a fully cooked white and a dry, crumbly yolk — with the spice dusting adding a subtle heat and colour. Nutritionally, hard-boiled eggs are one of the most nutrient-dense whole foods available: approximately 155 kcal per 100 g, with 12.6 g high-quality complete protein containing all essential amino acids, 10.6 g total fat (of which 3.27 g is saturated), and only 1.12 g carbohydrate, conferring a glycemic index of effectively zero. They are an excellent source of vitamin B12 (1.11 mcg/100 g), riboflavin (0.51 mg), vitamin A (149 mcg RAE), vitamin D (2.0 mcg), selenium (30.8 mcg), and choline. The dish is naturally gluten-free and suitable for halal diets. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the negligible carbohydrate content makes this an ideal protein source with no meaningful postprandial glucose impact. The primary clinical consideration is sodium from added salt (approximately 250 mg/100 g) relevant to hypertension management, and dietary cholesterol (373 mg/100 g) relevant to cardiovascular risk assessment. At a price of SGD 6.90 for two eggs, this dish is positioned as a protein side dish or snack within an Indian restaurant context in Singapore.
common_user_queries: Are boiled eggs good for diabetics?
How many calories in 2 boiled eggs?
Is boiled egg high in cholesterol?
Can I eat boiled eggs with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for hard-boiled eggs (FDC ID 173424), which is an exact match for the food preparation described. GI confirmed as effectively zero from University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al. 2008); glycemic_index set to 1 (sentinel for effectively zero GI) per Veritas convention. Ontology codes verified against FoodOn and FoodEx2 published vocabularies.
notes: 1. Serving size set to 100 g (approximately 2 medium eggs, ~50 g each edible weight) to align with the '2pcs' description in the input record. 2. Sodium value adjusted to 250 mg/100 g from the USDA base of 124 mg/100 g to reflect light salting as described in the dish description; this is a conservative estimate consistent with typical restaurant seasoning. 3. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). 4. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central; eggs contain no vitamin C (naturally zero). 5. glycemic_index is set to 1 (sentinel value for effectively zero GI) per Veritas convention for zero-GI foods; true GI is 0 as eggs contain negligible digestible carbohydrate. gi_category is 'low' (GI ≤ 55). glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 reflecting negligible carbohydrate load. 6. trans_fat_g of 0.04 g/100 g reflects naturally occurring vaccenic acid in egg yolk as reported by USDA; this is a naturally occurring trans fat, not an industrial trans fat. 7. All per_100g micronutrient fields are populated from USDA SR Legacy; no -1 sentinel values remain in this record. 8. Cuisine tagged as Indian per input source (Zaffron Tandoori, an Indian restaurant); multilingual names provided for English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish requirements. 9. FoodOn URI FOODON_03302184 verified as 'hard-boiled egg food product' in the FoodOn ontology. FoodEx2 code A00LC verified as 'Hen eggs, boiled' in the EFSA FoodEx2 classification system.
